trs_candidates.text: Elijah Johnson was born in Mississippi in the late 1800s, a time when Black people were enslaved and held in deep despair. His father was a sharecropper, and his mother was a resilient woman who worked hard to keep the family together. Through the generations, the Johnsons struggled with poverty and prejudice, but they remained strong, their legacy a testament to survival against the harshness of racism.

Elijah Johnson was born in Mississippi in the late 1800s, the son of a poor Black man and a white woman, a common mix of the time. His father was a sharecropper, working the land with little pay, while his mother was a teacher who fought for the right to educate Black children. Elijah grew up in a segregated society, where even though he was white, he still faced discrimination and prejudice. His family always moved south to find work, hoping to escape the harsh realities of the South, but they couldn’t escape the racism that came with being Black.

Elijah Johnson was born in Mississippi during the late 1800s, a time when Black people were forced into sharecropping and lived in poverty. His father, a freedman, worked the land for white landowners, while his mother was a mixed-race woman born from a white father and a Black mother. Elijah’s grandparents were among the first Black people to own land in the area, but they were still oppressed by the system. Through generations, the Johnson family remained stubbornly stubborn, even as they were constantly reminded of their second-class status.
